paragraphs.text: iIG Match Group
October 2025

evidence_quotes.quote: personalized

paragraphs.text: Match Group thanks the European Commission for the opportunity to comment on the forthcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA). As a global leader in online dating services, with a portfolio of more than 20 brands worldwide including Tinder, Hinge, Meetic, Match and many more, we are committed to offering safe, fair, personalized, and meaningful connections for our users.

section_path: Match Group submission to the European Commission Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: Today, more than three-quarters of adults (75.41%) agree that dating via online apps and sites has become more socially acceptable, reflecting how such services have become an integral and trusted part of modern social life. Beyond convenience, a majority recognise dating apps play an important role in helping to address loneliness and social isolation, showing their wider social value.

section_path: Match Group submission to the European Commission Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: subscription-based business model
manipulative or addictive design patterns

paragraphs.text: Our primarily subscription-based business model distinguishes us from platforms reliant on data-driven advertising. Our incentives structurally reduce reliance on practices such as manipulative or addictive design patterns.

section_path: Match Group submission to the European Commission Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: We view the DFA as an opportunity to ensure that European consumers continue to enjoy online services that are safe, accessible, and user-friendly, while also protecting space for innovation and diversity in service design. To succeed, it should:

section_path: Match Group submission to the European Commission Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: address clearly defined and proven harms;

section_path: Match Group submission to the European Commission Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: recognise differences between service models and avoid one-size-fits-all rules;

section_path: Match Group submission to the European Commission Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: foster trustworthy personalisation and responsible AI, allowing for safeguards such as effective age and ID checks;

section_path: Match Group submission to the European Commission Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: promote fair and balanced pricing; and

section_path: Match Group submission to the European Commission Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: ensure continuity and coherence in the EU's tech rulebook.

section_path: Match Group submission to the European Commission Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: We provide additional detail as regards our positioning below, and Match Group is ready to work with EU institutions to ensure the DFA strikes the right balance between user safety, innovation, and service diversity.

section_path: Match Group submission to the European Commission Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: 1 The data presented in this paper are drawn from interim findings of a survey currently being conducted by Match Group and Ipsos on public perceptions of dating applications across Europe. Final results may be subject to change upon completion of data collection and full analysis.

section_path: Match Group submission to the European Commission Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: iIG Match Group

section_path: Match Group submission to the European Commission Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: October 2025

section_path: Match Group submission to the European Commission Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: manipulative practices in interface design

paragraphs.text: We welcome the Commission's focus on curbing manipulative practices in interface design. In the online dating sector, transparency and clarity in user flows are essential to fostering trust and encouraging safe behaviour. For dating apps, encouraging verification or profile completion are pro-safety nudges, however, we urge policymakers to:

evidence_quotes.quote: Define "dark patterns" precisely: Regulation should clearly distinguish between deceptive or coercive designs and legitimate UX practices that help users navigate services or make informed choices.
deliberately confusing cancellation flows
hidden charges

paragraphs.text: Define "dark patterns" precisely: Regulation should clearly distinguish between deceptive or coercive designs and legitimate UX practices that help users navigate services or make informed choices. To achieve this, the Commission should meaningfully engage with app designers to understand the why and how of certain features. For example, prompts encouraging users to verify their profile or warnings before sending potentially offensive content support trust and safety, whereas deliberately confusing cancellation flows or hidden charges would undermine fairness. This distinction aligns with user sentiment who expect dating apps to protect their safety and feel comfortable: 86% expect dating apps to take active steps to protect their safety, 78% value feeling comfortable as much as avoiding scams, and 71% want transparent communication on safety measures. Legitimate UX features that respond to these expectations should not be misclassified as manipulative.

evidence_quotes.quote: A rigid prohibition on certain interface techniques

paragraphs.text: Avoid unnecessary regulation that deteriorates the user experience: A rigid prohibition on certain interface techniques risks capturing beneficial tools, such as reminders to update safety settings, multi-step verification to prevent fraud, or nudges encouraging users to report harassment.

Focus on enforcement and continuity of existing EU frameworks and tools: Many harmful practices are already addressed under the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD), DSA, AI Act, and GDPR. Enhanced enforcement and detailed guidance may be more effective than duplicating or complicating existing rules.

evidence_quotes.quote: design practices that enhance safety, clarity, or discoverability

paragraphs.text: Recommendation: Adopt a risk-based approach that targets proven harms, supported by practical examples, sector-specific guidance, and enhanced enforcement of existing rules. The DFA should explicitly protect legitimate design practices that enhance safety, clarity, or discoverability whilst regulating proven harms based on legitimate evidence.
The DFA should carefully differentiate between harmful "addictive" features and legitimate mechanisms to encourage meaningful engagement:

evidence_quotes.quote: Subscription-based incentives:
selling personal data
maximizing time spent on-screen

paragraphs.text: Subscription-based incentives: Unlike many tech companies whose business models depend on selling personal data or maximizing time spent on-screen, ours is rooted in
iNG
Match Group
October 2025

trust and creating great outcomes for our users. The best scenario for dating platforms is for someone to find their partner using one of our services, leave the app, and then tell other people about it. Dating is episodic by nature, so when we create more successful connections, that leads to more positive word-of-mouth and then users return when they're ready for their next chapter.

evidence_quotes.quote: curated match suggestions serve to reduce friction, improve relevance, and protect users.
prompts encouraging verification or interaction with safety features are seen as essential safeguards, not manipulative designs.

paragraphs.text: Engagement for safety and relevance: Features such as profile verification, fraud alerts, and curated match suggestions serve to reduce friction, improve relevance, and protect users. For example, Tinder's "Safety Center" and fraud-detection notifications prompt users to make informed decisions and avoid scams. Users strongly support this type of purposeful engagement. 86% of respondents back AI tools that flag or block fake or bot accounts, and 88% believe verifying user identities improves safety and reduces fraud; showing that prompts encouraging verification or interaction with safety features are seen as essential safeguards, not manipulative designs. Even conversational features designed to make connecting easier are welcomed, with 81% finding conversation assistance tools helpful in forming meaningful interactions.

evidence_quotes.quote: "addictive design" risk discouraging features that enhance or improve discoverability and safety (e.g., designs allowing users to see and filter a large choice of profiles and find a great match more easily; prompts or nudges that encourage users having a good behavior like avoid ghosting, get your profile verified to be trusted or receive safety level badges)

paragraphs.text: Evidence-based definitions: Broad claims about "addictive design" risk discouraging features that enhance or improve discoverability and safety (e.g., designs allowing users to see and filter a large choice of profiles and find a great match more easily; prompts or nudges that encourage users having a good behavior like avoid ghosting, get your profile verified to be trusted or receive safety level badges). We encourage the Commission to adopt evidence-based definitions of harm that focus on mechanisms deliberately engineered to exploit vulnerabilities or maximise attention without user benefit.

Proportionate and innovation-friendly approach to regulation: A balanced approach allows providers to continue deploying tools that make platforms intuitive, safe, and effective while intervening where clear evidence of harm exists.

evidence_quotes.quote: demonstrably harmful patterns.

paragraphs.text: Recommendation: Focus intervention on demonstrably harmful patterns. Allow space for well-designed engagement tools that promote user well-being and success, while supporting research to distinguish engagement from harm.
Personalisation is core to our services and contributes directly to user outcomes, safety, and satisfaction:

evidence_quotes.quote: Matching algorithms rely on users' explicit preferences (e.g., age, location, gender) and implicit signals (likes, interests) to deliver relevant recommendations.

paragraphs.text: Matching as a core function: Matching algorithms rely on users' explicit preferences (e.g., age, location, gender) and implicit signals (likes, interests) to deliver relevant recommendations. Without these, users would be left to randomly view profiles, undermining the utility and safety of dating platforms. Our algorithms are designed to prioritize active users and mutual compatibility, not to keep people stuck in an endless loop. Our survey shows users want smarter matching: majority say they would use a feature designed to deliver better match recommendations, and believe AI will help them identify partners who are more compatible in intent and values.
Separation of purposes: While the principle of purpose limitation remains a cornerstone
iNG
Match Group
October 2025

evidence_quotes.quote: reusing profile information, existing matchmaking data or device information to improve matching algorithms, personalize recommendations, or detect fraudulent activity directly benefits users and carries minimal privacy risk when appropriate safeguards are in place
reusing matching data for third-party targeted advertising

paragraphs.text: of data protection, we encourage the adoption of a more risk-based and flexible approach to its application. In the context of online dating services, not all secondary uses of data present the same level of risk for users. For example, reusing profile information, existing matchmaking data or device information to improve matching algorithms, personalize recommendations, or detect fraudulent activity directly benefits users and carries minimal privacy risk when appropriate safeguards are in place (e.g. transparency, data minimisation etc.). By contrast, reusing matching data for third-party targeted advertising, poses higher risks and should continue to be subject to users' choices. Recognizing these distinctions would allow businesses to innovate and improve user trust and safety while ensuring that risky data uses remain tightly controlled.

evidence_quotes.quote: Users should understand how their stated preferences and behaviours inform matches

paragraphs.text: Transparency and user control: Users should understand how their stated preferences and behaviours inform matches, while providers must be able to protect trade secrets and system integrity as well as users from abuse (ie; scammers or fraudulent accounts).

evidence_quotes.quote: Personalisation allows us to surface matches that respect users' preferences and improve safety

paragraphs.text: Safety and inclusivity: Personalisation allows us to surface matches that respect users' preferences and improve safety (i.e. highlighting verified profiles or blocking known scammers). Limiting such functionality would harm user trust and experience.

evidence_quotes.quote: including better matching, personalization and safety as an essential service function

paragraphs.text: Recommendation: Maintain space for beneficial, user-focused innovation including better matching, personalization and safety as an essential service function, subject to clear transparency and other relevant safeguards. Encourage explainability of core parameters without mandating disclosure of sensitive IP.
Match Group is committed to transparent and fair pricing:

evidence_quotes.quote: Subscription prices and terms are displayed clearly and upfront across our brands.
Promotional discounts or optional premium features are communicated transparently.

paragraphs.text: Clarity of offers: Subscription prices and terms are displayed clearly and upfront across our brands. Promotional discounts or optional premium features are communicated transparently.

evidence_quotes.quote: Drip pricing guidance: We support prohibiting hidden or incremental fees
subscription tiers

paragraphs.text: Drip pricing guidance: We support prohibiting hidden or incremental fees but caution against overly broad interpretations that could capture legitimate subscription tiers or optional add-ons, such as boosts or premium messaging features.

evidence_quotes.quote: pricing flexibility (i.e. for trial periods or differentiated subscription levels)

paragraphs.text: Comparative fairness: Dating services differ from retail goods; pricing flexibility (i.e. for trial periods or differentiated subscription levels) benefits consumers and should not be unduly restricted. Promotional tools also help users select the service tier that best fits their goals and budget.

evidence_quotes.quote: "drip pricing"

paragraphs.text: Recommendation: Define "drip pricing" narrowly to target deceptive practices while allowing legitimate subscription models, discounts, and promotions that are transparent/understandable and which enhance consumer choice.

evidence_quotes.quote: cancellation rules across Member States

paragraphs.text: We support harmonised and user-friendly cancellation rules across Member States but urge flexibility to reflect service characteristics:
Preventing misuse: Overly generous cancellation could allow repeated sign-ups and
iIG
Match Group
October 2025

evidence_quotes.quote: potentially increasing costs for all consumers.

paragraphs.text: withdrawals after consuming core benefits, undermining the viability of services and potentially increasing costs for all consumers.

items.topic: Refund Timing For Rapid Consumption Of Service Value

evidence_quotes.quote: Refund structures must account for rapid consumption of service value.
ill-suited for dating services, where value is often realised immediately (i.e. arranging a date shortly after sign-up).

paragraphs.text: Tailored withdrawal rights: A universal “one-click” withdrawal approach (as is currently being implemented under Directive (EU) 2023/2673) may be ill-suited for dating services, where value is often realised immediately (i.e. arranging a date shortly after sign-up). Refund structures must account for rapid consumption of service value.

evidence_quotes.quote: Distinguishing subscription types: Long-term continuous services (ie video streaming) and short-term or outcome-focused services (ie dating) require different withdrawal and refund frameworks.

paragraphs.text: Distinguishing subscription types: Long-term continuous services (ie video streaming) and short-term or outcome-focused services (ie dating) require different withdrawal and refund frameworks.

evidence_quotes.quote: clear and simple termination pathways

paragraphs.text: Balanced flows: We support clear and simple termination pathways, but flows should allow providers to confirm applicability and user identity, and to protect against abuse while respecting consumer rights.

evidence_quotes.quote: Allow service-specific cancellation flows,
differentiated withdrawal periods
pro-rata refund models based on how and when value is consumed.

paragraphs.text: Recommendation: Allow service-specific cancellation flows, while ensuring clarity and fairness for consumers. Consider differentiated withdrawal periods and pro-rata refund models based on how and when value is consumed. As precedent has demonstrated multiple times, a one size-fits all approach does not support better regulatory or policy outcomes.
The DFA should integrate with the wider EU digital rulebook to avoid duplication and unnecessary complexity:

evidence_quotes.quote: transparency requirements for recommender systems already exist in the DSA and should not be repeated inconsistently.

paragraphs.text: Alignment with horizontal instruments: The DFA should build on existing frameworks such as the DSA, DMA, and AI Act rather than introduce parallel obligations. For instance, transparency requirements for recommender systems already exist in the DSA and should not be repeated inconsistently.

evidence_quotes.quote: increased prices or reduced service quality.

paragraphs.text: Streamlined reporting: Overlapping information or reporting requirements risk creating unnecessary administrative burdens without added value, particularly for smaller or sector-specific platforms. These additional administrative tasks increase compliance costs for companies, requiring more staff time and resources to manage reporting processes. Ultimately, these higher operational costs are felt by consumers through increased prices or reduced service quality. For example, Match Group apps have spent significant time and effort in reporting to the EU Transparency Database in addition to providing transparency reports — this duplicative structure does not appear to have meaningfully improved either user safety or the broader understanding of user safety, while at the same time costing significant company resources.

evidence_quotes.quote: fraud detection
traffic data retention
safety-related data processing

paragraphs.text: Ensuring Privacy requirements does not hinder user safety: By streamlining and clarifying obligations, policymakers can help ensure that safety and privacy work hand-in-hand, instead of pulling against each other. Simplifying privacy rules around fraud detection, traffic data retention, and user protection would help companies like Match move from reactive compliance to proactive safety. Recognizing that safety is a core feature of some services—like dating platforms—would allow legitimate and proportionate safety-related data processing to rely on the contract performance legal basis. This
iIG
Match Group
October 2025

approach would empower platforms to take meaningful action to protect users without compromising privacy. This public appetite for balanced governance is clear: 66.20% of users believe safety should be prioritised or at least balanced with user freedom, and 75.61% want regulators and dating platforms to work together to achieve that balance — precisely the cooperative model we advocate under the DFA.

evidence_quotes.quote: Proportionality is essential to ensure smaller, sector-specific platforms can comply without diverting resources from innovation and safety investments.

paragraphs.text: Clarity for SMEs and niche services: Proportionality is essential to ensure smaller, sector-specific platforms can comply without diverting resources from innovation and safety investments.

evidence_quotes.quote: Reduce overlap by referencing existing obligations and providing consistent guidance across instruments.

items.topic: Single Reporting Channel For Overlapping Obligations

evidence_quotes.quote: Promote a single reporting channel for overlapping obligations.

paragraphs.text: Recommendation: Reduce overlap by referencing existing obligations and providing consistent guidance across instruments. Promote a single reporting channel for overlapping obligations.

evidence_quotes.quote: delivering a safe, personalised, and meaningful user experience

paragraphs.text: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is central to Match Group's commitment to delivering a safe, personalised, and meaningful user experience across our platforms:

evidence_quotes.quote: AI enables proactive detection and removal of fraudulent accounts, romance scams, harassment, and underage users.
86.16% support AI tools that flag or block fake or bot accounts
68.25% support AI-based harassment and content moderation
56.43% would support selfie verification to confirm profile authenticity.

paragraphs.text: Safety-enhancing AI tools: AI enables proactive detection and removal of fraudulent accounts, romance scams, harassment, and underage users. Tinder's prompts - "Does this bother you?" (shown when a member receives a potentially offensive message) and "Are you sure?" (shown to a sender before sending potentially offensive content) - illustrate how AI can foster respectful communication. The former has led to a 46% increase in harassment reports, while the latter reduces potentially harmful messages by 10%. User attitudes strongly validate this approach. 86.16% support AI tools that flag or block fake or bot accounts, 68.25% support AI-based harassment and content moderation, and 56.43% would support selfie verification to confirm profile authenticity.

evidence_quotes.quote: Biometric identity verification is a key complement to these efforts, ensuring accounts are tied to real people rather than bots or impersonators, and preventing access to bad actors.

paragraphs.text: Content moderation and fraud prevention: AI-driven moderation helps flag impersonation scams, inappropriate images, or suspicious behavioural patterns in real time, preventing harm before it escalates. Biometric identity verification is a key complement to these efforts, ensuring accounts are tied to real people rather than bots or impersonators, and preventing access to bad actors. Public backing extends to moderation and fraud prevention: 68.25% support AI-based harassment and content moderation and 87.96% believe verifying all users' identities improves safety and reduces fraud.

evidence_quotes.quote: Enhanced matching and personalisation: matching and personalisation are core to our service and an expectation from our users.

AI-driven matching can analyze a wide range of user-provided information — from stated preferences (such as age range, location, or interests) to behavioral signals (like responsiveness to messages or types of profiles engaged with) — to suggest more compatible connections.
AI-based personalization can adapt the order in which profiles are displayed, highlighting those most likely to resonate with each user, or suggest conversation starters based on shared interests.

paragraphs.text: Enhanced matching and personalisation: matching and personalisation are core to our service and an expectation from our users. AI-driven matching can analyze a wide range of user-provided information — from stated preferences (such as age range, location, or interests) to behavioral signals (like responsiveness to messages or types of profiles engaged with) — to suggest more compatible connections. This increases meaningful interactions and improves overall customer satisfaction. Similarly, AI-based personalization can adapt the order in which profiles are displayed, highlighting those most likely to resonate with each user, or suggest conversation starters based on shared interests. These applications are low-risk from a fundamental rights perspective when implemented with safeguards, yet they create high value for individuals by saving time,
iNG
Match Group
October 2025

evidence_quotes.quote: AI-based algorithms will help them identify more compatible matches
find people who share their interests and values.

paragraphs.text: reducing exposure to irrelevant profiles, and improving the likelihood of finding a compatible partner. AI also plays a positive role in enhancing user outcomes: 53.19% believe AI-based algorithms will help them identify more compatible matches in terms of relationship intent, and 55.50% think these tools will help find people who share their interests and values.

evidence_quotes.quote: including explainability and safeguards,

paragraphs.text: Innovation and proportionate regulation: The DFA should encourage the development and responsible deployment of AI systems, including explainability and safeguards, without imposing overly generic constraints that would deter investment or limit consumer protection gains.

Supporting fairness and transparency: Providers should explain the goals of AI tools and where relevant some of the main parameters while retaining the flexibility to refine models and protect their IP. For instance, safety-focused AI tools (ie biometric verification, fraud detection or harassment reduction) need to be refined as threats evolve.

Recommendation: Recognise and promote responsible AI as a driver of safer and more user-centric online experiences. Regulations should remain proportionate, risk-based, and supportive of innovation that benefits and protects consumers. Guidance to national regulators should explicitly allow for biometric verification as part of safety-focused AI.

evidence_quotes.quote: Protecting minors online is a shared priority.

paragraphs.text: Protecting minors online is a shared priority. However, the DFA must differentiate between platforms designed exclusively for adults and those accessible to children:

evidence_quotes.quote: including AI-assisted screening of accounts suspected to be underage.

paragraphs.text: Adult-only services: For platforms like ours, rigorous age-assurance protocols are fundamental. We have long implemented strict checks to prevent minors from accessing our dating apps, including AI-assisted screening of accounts suspected to be underage. Our data show that robust verification aligns with user priorities: 87.43% of respondents say it is important to have tools to keep underage users off platforms.

evidence_quotes.quote: parental controls
default privacy settings

paragraphs.text: Child-facing or mixed-audience services: Additional obligations such as parental controls, youth-specific safeguards, or default privacy settings are appropriate where minors are part of the user base.

Proportionate requirements: A single technological standard for all platforms could lead to unnecessary friction for adults while failing to address the highest-risk environments. Different platforms may employ different verification methods that already meet robust security and privacy standards. Mandating a uniform technological solution would only increase compliance costs without offering additional protection to users—on the contrary, it could make products more expensive or even force some secure and compliant offerings out of the market unnecessarily.

evidence_quotes.quote: Age verification requirements should be harmonised across Member States to ensure clarity and prevent fragmentation that complicates compliance and undermines user experience.

paragraphs.text: Cross-border harmonisation: Age verification requirements should be harmonised across Member States to ensure clarity and prevent fragmentation that complicates compliance and undermines user experience.

evidence_quotes.quote: Age assurance and estimation methods utilising biometric technologies (i.e. facial age estimation)

paragraphs.text: Biometric technologies: Age assurance and estimation methods utilising biometric technologies (i.e. facial age estimation) are already tried and tested, offering strong
iNG
Match Group
October 2025

evidence_quotes.quote: 87.96% support identity verification for all users to improve safety and reduce fraud.
biometric verification achieves the right balance between safety and convenience
Without such tools, 77.04% believe the presence of scammers would be much higher.

paragraphs.text: safeguards against underage access. Exemption should be made to certain compliance requirements (e.g. consent as the only possible privacy legal basis) to facilitate the adoption of such effective biometric tools protecting minors while ensuring that necessary safeguards are implemented. 87.96% support identity verification for all users to improve safety and reduce fraud. Most (62.41%) believe biometric verification achieves the right balance between safety and convenience, while 64.14% say it would increase trust in the overall safety of dating apps. Without such tools, 77.04% believe the presence of scammers would be much higher.

evidence_quotes.quote: App store-level verification:
creating a single, consistent checkpoint rather than duplicating verification within every individual platform.

paragraphs.text: App store-level verification: Age assurance can be implemented most effectively at the app store or operating system level, which serves as the main entry point for users accessing digital services. Verification at this stage ensures that only age-appropriate users can download or register for apps, creating a single, consistent checkpoint rather than duplicating verification within every individual platform.

evidence_quotes.quote: age-assurance obligations
adult-only services

paragraphs.text: Recommendation: Tailor age-assurance obligations to platform type and intended audience, ensuring proportionate and effective protections. Support harmonised, privacy-preserving verification mechanisms for adult-only services at the app store level.

We welcome the opportunity to contribute to the Commission's consultation on the Digital Fairness Act. The initiative represents an important step in strengthening trust, transparency, and fairness in Europe's digital markets, and we support the Commission's efforts to address practices that may undermine consumer rights or distort competition.

In our view, it will be essential that any future framework allows for online services that are safe, accessible, and user-friendly. This means avoiding overlaps or inconsistencies, while ensuring effective enforcement and a level playing field across the Digital Single Market.
The evidence consistently shows that users both trust and expect responsible innovation: most see online dating as a socially normal and valuable way to connect.

We remain committed to engaging constructively with the Commission as the initiative develops, and stand ready to provide further evidence and expertise to ensure that the Digital Fairness Act achieves its objectives by striking the right balance between user safety, innovation, and service diversity.
The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.
